text: Chapter 1 establishes the foundational premise of the method: that jazz language can be learned and taught through a finite, prescriptive set of core devices that every student should master in order to communicate fluently with other jazz musicians. The book identifies eighteen devices as the common vocabulary shared across all studied jazz players, with these elements accounting for nearly all the language used in the tradition. The core named elements introduced here are digital patterns, the bebop scale, the bebop lick, and enclosures—foundational terminology that organizes the subsequent instructional chapters. The chapter's theoretical framework rests on the claim that a certain grouping of these ideas and approaches, when consistently applied, gives an identity to a style, making jazz language learnable and reproducible rather than ineffable or purely intuitive.
